quiz.title: SAT English: Unit 5 Quiz 1
quiz.subtitle: Goal-Oriented Writing & Vocabulary-in-Context — Selecting the Option That Best Accomplishes a Specific Task (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: SAT Preparation

questions.question:
*Adapted from a paper on marine biology.*

The 'recruitment' of coral larvae to a degraded reef is a complex process influenced by water temperature, chemical signals from existing algae, and the presence of suitable limestone substrates. Scientists have observed that even when adult colonies are healthy, a reef may fail to recover if the local environment does not support the settlement and growth of these microscopic pioneers. Understanding the factors that facilitate this replenishment is critical for developing effective reef restoration strategies in the face of climate change.

As used in the passage, what does the word 'recruitment' most nearly mean?
options: The act of enlisting individuals to fight and defend a species
The process through which organisms grow and adapt in a habitat
The process of new organisms joining a population
The act of reproducing profusely within a specific region
questions.explanation: Choice C is correct because in ecological terms, recruitment refers to the successful addition of new individuals to a population. Choice A is a thematic distractor that uses the common military definition of 'enlisting.' Choice B is a general biological distractor that describes growth rather than the specific act of 'joining' or 'settling.' Choice D describes reproduction, but the passage focuses on the 'settlement' of larvae that have already been produced.

questions.question:
*Adapted from a treatise on macroeconomics.*

When a central bank decides to 'contract' the money supply, it typically does so to combat rising inflation. By increasing interest rates, the bank makes borrowing more expensive, which effectively slows down consumer spending and business investment. While this move can successfully stabilize prices, it also carries the risk of dampening economic growth, potentially leading to a period of stagnation if the tightening of credit is too aggressive or poorly timed.

As used in the passage, what does the word 'contract' most nearly mean?
options: To enter into a formal legal agreement
To produce a lot of money
To decide how much money should be produced
To reduce in size or volume
questions.explanation: Choice D is correct because in economics, to 'contract' means to shrink or decrease the available supply of money. Choice A uses the common legal definition of a 'contract,' which is irrelevant here. Choices B and C are distractors that sound plausible in a banking context (money production) but incorrectly define the word 'contract' itself.

questions.question:
*Adapted from a history of architectural engineering.*

The use of 'flying buttresses' in Gothic cathedrals was a revolutionary solution to the problem of lateral force. Unlike earlier Romanesque structures that relied on massive, thick walls to support the weight of the roof, the Gothic style diverted the 'thrust' of the heavy stone vaults outward and downward through these external arches. This innovation allowed for the installation of soaring stained-glass windows, as the walls themselves were no longer required to bear the full burden of the building's structural integrity.

As used in the passage, what does the word 'thrust' most nearly mean?
options: A sudden or violent physical lunge
The outward force exerted by a structure
The very heavy weight of the stone vault
The structure that absorbs vibration easily
questions.explanation: Choice B is correct because 'thrust' in an architectural context refers to the outward or lateral pressure exerted by an arch or vault. Choice A is a common literal definition of the word used as a physical movement. Choice C is a thematic distractor; while the 'weight' of the vault is mentioned in the text, it is the source of the pressure rather than the definition of the pressure itself. Choice D is a technical-sounding distractor that introduces the unrelated concept of vibrations.

questions.question:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
  • The 'Kintsugi' technique is a Japanese art form for repairing broken pottery.
  • Artisans use lacquer mixed with powdered gold, silver, or platinum to join ceramic pieces.
  • The philosophy behind Kintsugi is to treat breakage and repair as part of the history of an object.
  • Instead of hiding the cracks, the technique highlights them, making the repaired item more beautiful than the original.
  • The practice dates back to the late 15th century.

The student wants to explain the philosophy of Kintsugi. Which choice most effectively uses information from the given notes to accomplish this goal?
options: The philosophy of Kintsugi holds that an object’s history, including its breakage, should be highlighted rather than hidden.
Kintsugi is a Japanese art form dating back to the late 15th century that uses precious metals to repair pottery.
In Kintsugi, artisans use lacquer mixed with powdered gold or silver to join broken ceramic pieces back together.
Kintsugi practitioners make repaired items more beautiful than the original by using platinum and other precious metals.
questions.explanation: Choice A is correct because it directly addresses the 'philosophy' (highlighting history/breakage) as requested by the goal. Choice B and C provide historical and technical details but ignore the philosophical aspect. Choice D mentions the result of the philosophy but focuses more on the materials used.

questions.question:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
  • The 'Svalbard Global Seed Vault' is located on a remote island in Norway.
  • It is designed to store duplicates of seed samples from the world's crop collections.
  • The facility is built 120 meters deep inside a sandstone mountain.
  • It is nicknamed the 'Doomsday Vault' because it ensures food security in the event of a global crisis.
  • The permafrost and thick rock ensure that the seed samples remain frozen even without electricity.

The student wants to emphasize the vault’s capacity for long-term preservation. Which choice most effectively uses information from the given notes to accomplish this goal?
options: Located in Norway, the Svalbard Global Seed Vault is a remote facility used to store seed duplicates from around the world.
The 'Doomsday Vault' was built to protect global food security during major crises by storing crop samples.
The vault’s location deep inside a mountain ensures that seeds remain frozen through permafrost, even if power is lost.
Built 120 meters into a sandstone mountain, the Svalbard Global Seed Vault is the world's most remote storage facility.
questions.explanation: Choice C is correct because it focuses on the mechanics of preservation (frozen seeds via permafrost/rock, even without electricity). Choice A and D focus on the location and depth without explicitly linking them to how the seeds are preserved long-term. Choice B focuses on the purpose (food security) rather than the mechanism of preservation.

questions.question:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
  • The Voyager 1 spacecraft was launched by NASA in 1977.
  • It carries a 'Golden Record' containing sounds and images selected to portray the diversity of life on Earth.
  • In 2012, Voyager 1 became the first human-made object to enter interstellar space.
  • It is currently more than 14 billion miles away from Earth.
  • The spacecraft continues to transmit data via the Deep Space Network.

The student wants to highlight the current status of the Voyager 1 mission. Which choice most effectively uses information from the given notes to accomplish this goal?
options: Launched in 1977, Voyager 1 carries a 'Golden Record' intended for any extraterrestrial life it might encounter.
Currently over 14 billion miles away, Voyager 1 continues to transmit data from interstellar space to Earth.
Voyager 1, the first object to reach interstellar space, carries images that portray the diversity of life on Earth.
The Voyager 1 mission began in 1977 and involves a spacecraft that is now billions of miles away from our planet.
questions.explanation: Choice B is correct because it highlights the 'current status' (current distance and ongoing data transmission). Choice A and C focus on the launch and the Golden Record, which are historical details. Choice D is less specific than B regarding the actual distance and current activities of the spacecraft.

questions.question:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
  • The 'Glass Octopus' (*Vitreledonella richardi*) is a nearly transparent species.
  • It lives in the 'midnight zone' of the ocean, thousands of feet below the surface.
  • Its internal organs are visible through its clear skin.
  • This transparency serves as a form of camouflage in the dimly lit waters.

The student wants to combine the notes into a single sentence that explains how the Glass Octopus's appearance helps it survive. Which choice most effectively uses information from the given notes to accomplish this goal?
options: The nearly transparent skin of the Glass Octopus allows it to survive in the midnight zone by providing camouflage.
Because the Glass Octopus is nearly transparent, its internal organs are visible even in the deep midnight zone.
The Glass Octopus lives thousands of feet below the surface, where it uses camouflage to hide its internal organs.
A nearly transparent species known as the Glass Octopus has clear skin and lives in the dimly lit midnight zone.
questions.explanation: Choice A is correct because it creates a single, coherent sentence that directly links the physical trait (transparency) to the survival benefit (camouflage). Choice B focuses on the visibility of organs rather than survival. Choice C implies the octopus is hiding its organs *specifically*, rather than using its whole body for camouflage. Choice D is a list of facts that lacks the explanatory 'how' requested in the goal.

questions.question:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
  • The Great Library of Alexandria was founded in Egypt around 300 BCE.
  • It aimed to house the entirety of human knowledge.
  • Scholars traveled from across the Mediterranean to study its collection of papyrus scrolls.
  • The library was eventually destroyed, resulting in the loss of countless ancient texts.

The student wants to combine the notes to emphasize the library's historical mission and its ultimate fate. Which choice most effectively uses information from the given notes to accomplish this goal?
options: Founded in 300 BCE, the Great Library of Alexandria was a center for scholars who studied ancient papyrus scrolls.
After scholars traveled across the Mediterranean to study there, the Great Library of Alexandria was destroyed in Egypt.
The loss of countless ancient texts occurred when the Great Library of Alexandria, which housed papyrus scrolls, was destroyed.
The Great Library of Alexandria sought to house all human knowledge, but its eventual destruction led to the loss of countless texts.
questions.explanation: Choice D is correct because it successfully pairs the 'mission' (housing all knowledge) with the 'fate' (destruction/loss of texts) using the contrast word 'but.' Choice A omits the fate. Choice B omits the mission. Choice C focuses on the texts rather than the library's specific mission as described in the notes.

questions.question:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
  • Glow-worms (*Arachnocampa luminosa*) are found in the caves of New Zealand.
  • They produce a bioluminescent blue-green light from their abdomens.
  • The light attracts small insects into sticky silk threads spun by the larvae.
  • This method allows the glow-worms to feed in the absolute darkness of the caves.

The student wants to combine the notes into a single sentence that describes the glow-worm's hunting mechanism. Which choice most effectively uses information from the given notes to accomplish this goal?
options: Glow-worms live in the absolute darkness of New Zealand caves and produce a blue-green light from their abdomens.
The sticky silk threads spun by glow-worm larvae are used to catch insects that live in the dark caves of New Zealand.
By producing a bioluminescent light, glow-worms in New Zealand caves attract insects into sticky silk threads to feed.
Found in New Zealand, glow-worms use bioluminescence and silk threads to survive in the darkness of caves.
questions.explanation: Choice C is correct because it synthesizes all parts of the hunting mechanism (the light, the attraction, and the silk threads) into a single logical flow. Choice A describes the environment and the light but omits the silk threads and the 'hunting' aspect. Choice B omits the bioluminescence. Choice D is too vague about how the tools are used together.

questions.question:
While researching a topic, a student has taken the following notes:
  • The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) is the most powerful space telescope ever built.
  • It primarily observes the universe in the infrared spectrum.
  • The Hubble Space Telescope, launched in 1990, observes mainly in visible and ultraviolet light.
  • Infrared light allows the JWST to peer through dense dust clouds that obscure visible light.
  • This capability enables the telescope to see the very first stars and galaxies formed after the Big Bang.

The student wants to explain the advantage of the JWST's infrared capabilities. Which choice most effectively uses information from the given notes to accomplish this goal?
options: The JWST is more powerful than the Hubble Space Telescope, which was launched by NASA in 1990.
Observing in the infrared spectrum allows the JWST to see through dust clouds to study the universe's earliest stars.
Unlike the Hubble, which uses visible light, the JWST is a modern telescope that uses infrared light.
The JWST can detect galaxies that formed after the Big Bang, making it the most powerful telescope in history.
questions.explanation: Choice B is correct because it explicitly connects the capability (infrared) to the specific advantage (seeing through dust clouds to view early stars). Choice A and C focus on a comparison with Hubble or general technical facts. Choice D mentions the result (seeing early galaxies) but omits the 'how'—the infrared capability mentioned in the goal.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can accurately select the option that best accomplishes a specific rhetorical goal using provided source material.
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review missed questions and focus on whether the choice directly addresses the stated goal or includes irrelevant information.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Remember: rhetorical synthesis questions test your ability to match content to purpose. Eliminate options that miss the goal.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Ask yourself: what is the specific task, and which option uses the notes most effectively to complete it?
